message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I'm implementing deep-merge in Features.update(). Only need features.py. What are you working on?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I'm implementing a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator on the Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding automatically. Please let me know your files so we can avoid overlap.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py (only file for our features). My change is small and isolated: just the Features.update() method (deep-merge dicts). Your decorator sounds like it touches __init__/other methods, not update(). Proposal: I'll make my edit first (quick, isolated), send DONE, then you pull latest and layer your decorator on top. Does update() overlap with your decorator work? If not, ack and I'll proceed.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both only need features.py (no other files), so a disjoint split isn't possible. Proposal: serialize instead. My change wraps __setitem__/__delitem__/update with a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to auto-sync _column_requires_decoding - it's a structural/mechanical change to those dunder methods. I'll go first and send DONE when finished (should be quick). Then you layer your deep-merge logic inside the (now-decorated) update() method on top of my version. This avoids us clobbering each other's edits to the same methods. OK?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed - you go first with the decorator/sync changes, send DONE, then I'll layer deep-merge logic inside update(). Waiting for your DONE now.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Overlap confirmed: my decorator wraps __setitem__, __delitem__, AND update() specifically to auto-sync _column_requires_decoding - so update() is exactly where we'd collide. My change is mechanical/structural (turns update() into a decorated method, replacing the current 8-line manual-sync body with a thin call to the wrapped dict.update, or similar), not touching merge semantics. Let me land first since your deep-merge logic needs to go INSIDE whatever update() body exists afterward. I'll keep it minimal and send DONE shortly - then please pull the latest features.py and add your deep-merge logic inside the post-decorator update() method. Proceeding now unless you object.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Confirmed - implementing now, will send DONE shortly.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Landed keep_features_dicts_synced decorator + applied it to __setitem__, __delitem__, update, setdefault, pop, popitem, clear (as class-level dict-method assignments, not custom def bodies). update() is now literally 'update = keep_features_dicts_synced(dict.update)' - if you want to add deep-merge logic, you'll need to change it to a real 'def update(self, other, **kwargs): ...' function decorated with @keep_features_dicts_synced that implements the merge and then assigns into self (e.g. via self[key] = ... or dict.update(self, ...)), rather than plain dict.update. Tests pass (tests/features/test_features.py: 25 passed). Full features.py is yours now - go ahead.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Deep-merge logic added to Features.update() (recursive dict/Features merge + Sequence-with-dict-feature merge). Tests in tests/features/test_features.py pass (25 passed, 3 skipped). Wrote patch.txt from my working copy. Note: my filesystem shows no trace of your decorator changes (clean git diff before my edit) - we may be in separate sandboxes, so no actual file clobbering risk on my end regardless.
